worth_it_explanation: Campaign type decides how a campaign finds people and which auctions it enters, so two campaigns aimed at the same demand can collide. Performance Max and Search competing for one query, or a Search campaign running on the Display Network by default, both spend without a clear plan for which should win. The check reads the campaign types and their settings against the goals they serve. The fix is a settings or structure change rather than a rebuild, and it stops two parts of the account from bidding against each other.
intro: Google Ads offers several campaign types, and each reaches people a different way. Search matches typed queries, Performance Max spans Search, Shopping, Display, YouTube, and more from one campaign, and the Display Network shows image ads across sites. Problems appear when two types chase the same demand with no rule for which wins, or when one campaign runs on a network that does not fit its goal. These checks confirm that Performance Max and Search have a clear order on shared queries, and that Search campaigns are not spending on the Display Network by default.

audit_checks.title: Performance Max and Search compete for the same queries with no prioritization plan
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When both campaign types chase the same queries, Google's serving rules decide which shows, and the account may not realize Performance Max is taking traffic a Search campaign was built for. Spend and reporting split across two campaigns in ways the practitioner did not plan, which makes each harder to read and tune.

educate.base:
Performance Max and Search campaigns can both serve on the same query, so an account running both needs to know which one wins. Google's rule is that a Search campaign with a keyword that exactly matches, or is spell-corrected to match, the query is preferred over Performance Max. When no keyword matches the query exactly across match types, the campaign with the higher Ad Rank serves, and that can be Performance Max.

The practical effect is that Performance Max can take queries a Search campaign was built to own, especially when the Search campaign uses broader match types or is limited by budget. The account then splits spend and conversions across two campaigns in a way no one planned, and the Search campaign's reported performance drops without an obvious cause. The reverse also happens, where a thin Search campaign holds back Performance Max from queries it could convert.

A few signs point to an unplanned overlap:
  • Performance Max and Search campaigns target the same products or services with no agreed division
  • A Search campaign's impressions or conversions fell after Performance Max launched
  • The same query appears in both the Search terms report and the Performance Max search categories
Deciding which campaign should own which demand, then shaping match types, budgets, and exclusions to match, restores a clear plan. You may want to map each campaign to the demand it should serve before changing settings, so the division reflects the goals rather than the current accident.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, Performance Max and Standard Shopping can both serve the catalog, and Performance Max is preferred over Standard Shopping for the same products by default. You may want to confirm the two are not running the same inventory without a deliberate reason, so the catalog spend is not split unpredictably.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, a Search campaign on high-intent queries and a broad Performance Max campaign can pull the same searches. You may want to confirm the high-intent queries are protected by exact-match Search keywords so Performance Max does not absorb them.

learn_more.source_title: How Performance Max interacts with other campaigns in your account

investigate.intro: Confirm whether Performance Max and Search target the same demand and which campaign is set to win shared queries.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: List the active campaigns and note which are Search and which are Performance Max.
For any pair that targets the same products or services, check the Search campaign's keyword match types and whether it is limited by budget.
Review the Search terms report and the Performance Max search categories for the same queries appearing in both. Shared queries with no plan for which campaign should own them is the pattern to flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether any Performance Max and Search campaigns target the same demand and whether a deliberate plan governs which wins shared queries. A clear division, or no overlap, passes. Overlap with no plan, where Performance Max can absorb queries a Search campaign was built for, is a fail recorded with the campaigns and the shared demand.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which campaigns are Performance Max and Search, and whether a plan governs shared queries, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs Performance Max and Search campaigns against the same demand with no plan for which should win shared queries. Because Search is preferred only on exact keyword matches and Performance Max can take the rest, spend and conversions split across the two in ways that were not planned, and each campaign gets harder to read and tune. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each campaign to the demand it should own, then shaping match types, budgets, and exclusions so the division is deliberate, and watching that the Search campaign holds the queries it was built for.

audit_checks.title: A Search campaign is opted into the Display Network, mixing two networks in one budget
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a Search campaign also serves on the Display Network, its budget covers two very different kinds of inventory, and the Display impressions convert and behave differently from search. The blend hides which network is working and often spends on low-intent Display placements at the expense of search.

educate.base:
A Search campaign can be set to also show on the Display Network, an option Google has presented as a way to reach more people. The trouble is that search and display are different kinds of inventory. Search reaches people typing a query with intent, while display shows image or text ads to people browsing other sites who did not search for anything. Putting both in one campaign means one budget and one bid strategy cover two behaviors that convert at very different rates.

In practice the Display side of such a campaign often spends on low-intent placements that convert poorly, and because the two networks share a campaign, the search performance and the display performance blend together in reporting. The account cannot easily see that the Display portion is dragging the average, and the search budget is diluted.

The campaign's network settings show whether Display is included. A few signs point to an unwanted network blend:
  • A Search campaign's settings show the Display Network turned on
  • The campaign's placements report lists websites and apps, not just search
  • Conversion rate on the campaign is well below other pure-search campaigns
Turning off the Display Network on Search campaigns, and running Display as its own campaign when it is wanted, keeps each network's budget and reporting clean. You may want to check the placements the Display portion served before turning it off, so any genuinely useful placement can be rebuilt deliberately in its own campaign.

site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, Display impressions rarely match the intent of someone searching for the service, so a Search campaign bleeding budget to Display usually raises cost per lead. You may want to separate the networks so the search budget funds the high-intent queries.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether any Search campaign is also serving on the Display Network.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open each Search campaign's settings and check the networks section.
Note whether the Display Network is included alongside search.
For any campaign with Display on, open its placements report and confirm it is serving on websites and apps. A Search campaign spending on Display placements is the pattern to flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Search campaign, whether the Display Network is included. A Search campaign limited to search passes. A Search campaign also serving on Display, blending two networks in one budget, is a fail recorded with the campaign and its Display placements.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each Search campaign, whether the Display Network is turned on to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs at least one Search campaign with the Display Network turned on, so one budget covers both search and display inventory. The Display portion reaches people who did not search and often converts poorly, and because the two networks share a campaign, the weak Display performance hides inside the campaign average and dilutes the search budget. {{voice_recommend}} turning off the Display Network on Search campaigns, reviewing the Display placements first in case any is worth keeping, and running Display as its own campaign when it is wanted.
